# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark-design evidence, dataset-construction details, evaluation metrics, comparative benchmark statistics, model performance results, and ablation findings about agentic coding on feature-level software development tasks.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: claim-support
citation: FeatureBench abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The paper states that existing agentic coding benchmarks mainly cover limited task scopes such as bug fixing within a single pull request.

## Source Excerpt

"Existing agentic coding benchmarks, however, cover a limited task scope, e.g., bug fixing within a single pull request (PR)"

## Note

This is a characterization of prior benchmarks made by the source.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: FeatureBench abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
FeatureBench uses an execution-based evaluation protocol and a test-driven collection method that automatically derives tasks from repositories with minimal human effort.

## Source Excerpt

"FeatureBench incorporates an execution-based evaluation protocol and a scalable test-driven method that automatically derives tasks from code repositories with minimal human effort."

## Note

The evidence directly describes how the benchmark is constructed and evaluated.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: FeatureBench abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The collection method traces unit tests along a dependency graph to identify feature-level tasks that can span multiple commits and pull requests.

## Source Excerpt

"By tracing from unit tests along a dependency graph, our approach can identify feature-level coding tasks spanning multiple commits and PRs scattered across the development timeline"

## Note

The statement describes the extraction mechanism used to construct tasks.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: FeatureBench abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The first version of FeatureBench contains 200 evaluation tasks and 3825 executable environments from 24 open-source repositories.

## Source Excerpt

"Using this framework, we curated 200 challenging evaluation tasks and 3825 executable environments from 24 open-source repositories in the first version of our benchmark."

## Note

This is a benchmark composition statistic reported by the source.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: FeatureBench abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
The paper reports that Claude 4.5 Opus achieves 74.4% resolved rate on SWE-bench but only 11.0% on FeatureBench.

## Source Excerpt

"Claude 4.5 Opus, which achieves a 74.4% resolved rate on SWE-bench, succeeds on only 11.0% of tasks"

## Note

This is a direct cross-benchmark performance comparison reported in the abstract.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: metric
citation: FeatureBench introduction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Using the Codex agent scaffold, GPT-5.1-Codex with medium reasoning resolved 12.5% of task cases.

## Source Excerpt

"Using the Codex agent framework, GPT-5.1-Codex (medium reasoning) successfully completes 12.5% of the task cases."

## Note

This is a reported baseline result on the benchmark.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: method
citation: FeatureBench Section 3.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Each problem prompt explicitly provides interface definitions, import paths, expected behaviors, and requires a directly callable solution.

## Source Excerpt

"Each prompt explicitly specifies the clear interface definitions, import paths, and the descriptions of expected behaviors, and enforces that the solution must be directly callable"

## Note

This is part of the benchmark's evaluation design.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: method
citation: FeatureBench Section 3.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Repository environment setup is the only manual step, takes about three minutes per repository, and totals less than one hour across 24 repositories.

## Source Excerpt

"we manually specify installation commands (taking approximately three minutes)" and "the total human labor required to complete this for all 24 repositories amounts to less than one hour"

## Note

This evidence describes the manual labor requirement in the collection pipeline.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: method
citation: FeatureBench Section 3.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The full benchmark configuration uses five pass-to-pass test files per environment.

## Source Excerpt

"Leveraging our pipelines, we configured the number of P2P test files to five"

## Note

This is a benchmark configuration detail for the full set.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: method
citation: FeatureBench Section 3.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Tasks were filtered to include only cases with more than 100 lines of pending implementation, at least 10 fail-to-pass test points, and test files initially committed after May 2022.

## Source Excerpt

"we restricted inclusion to tasks exceeding 100 lines of pending implementation, encompassing at least 10 F2P test points, with test files initially committed after May 2022"

## Note

This is a stated dataset filtering rule.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: FeatureBench introduction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The benchmark's first version contains tasks created from May 2022 through September 2025.

## Source Excerpt

"Using this pipeline, we have curated a benchmark with 200 evaluation instances and 3825 verifiable environments, created from May 2022 to September 2025"

## Note

This gives the creation-time range of benchmark instances.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Table 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
Average FeatureBench L1 problem texts are 4818.0 words long versus 195.1 words for SWE-bench.

## Source Excerpt

Table 3 reports "Problem Texts Length (Words) 195.1" for SWE-bench and "4818.0" for FeatureBench.

## Note

This is a quantitative task-complexity comparison between the two benchmarks.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: metric
citation: Table 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
Average gold solutions in FeatureBench L1 span 790.2 lines, 15.7 files, and 29.2 functions, versus 32.8 lines, 1.7 files, and 3 functions in SWE-bench.

## Source Excerpt

Table 3 lists "Gold Solution # Lines 32.8" vs "790.2", "# Files 1.7" vs "15.7", and "# Functions 3" vs "29.2" for SWE-bench and FeatureBench respectively.

## Note

This evidence combines related implementation-size metrics from the same comparison table.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: metric
citation: Table 4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
On the shared-repository subset, Claude Opus 4.5 resolved 74.40% of SWE-bench Verified tasks but 5.2% of FeatureBench subset tasks.

## Source Excerpt

Table 4 reports for "Claude Opus 4.5" a "% Resolved" of "74.40" on SWE-bench Verified and "5.2" on the FeatureBench subset.

## Note

This is a direct subset comparison intended by the source to align repository coverage.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Table 7
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Removing explicit interface information reduced Lite-set performance: Gemini-3-Pro-Preview fell from 10.0% to 3.3% resolved, and GPT-5.1-Codex fell from 20.0% to 16.7% resolved.

## Source Excerpt

Under "Without Interface" in Table 7, Gemini-3-Pro-Preview shows "3.3 (-6.7)" resolved and GPT-5.1-Codex shows "16.7 (-3.3)"; the comparison baseline in the same table implies 10.0% and 20.0% resolved with interface visible.

## Note

The evidence supports the paper's claim that explicit interfaces materially affect performance.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: metric
citation: Table 7
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Providing visible unit tests sharply increased Lite-set performance: GPT-5.1-Codex reached 63.3% resolved and 80.9% passed, while Gemini-3-Pro-Preview reached 60.0% resolved and 80.6% passed.

## Source Excerpt

In Table 7 under "Visible Unit Tests," GPT-5.1-Codex reports "63.3 (+43.3)" resolved and "80.9 (+20.7)" passed, while Gemini-3-Pro-Preview reports "60.0 (+50.0)" resolved and "80.6 (+37.2)" passed.

## Note

This evidence shows the measured effect of giving agents ground-truth unit tests.
```

### file: evidence-17.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-17
kind: metric
citation: Table 9
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The LLM classifier for identifying top-level tested objects achieved 81.03% precision, 89.24% recall, 84.94% F1, and 91.74% accuracy.

## Source Excerpt

Table 9 reports "Precision 81.03% Recall 89.24% F1 Score 84.94% Accuracy 91.74%."

## Note

This is a quantitative evaluation of one component in the data construction pipeline.
```

### file: evidence-18.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-18
kind: claim-support
citation: FeatureBench Section 4.1.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The authors interpret the dominance of NameError in Claude Opus 4.5 failures as evidence that models still struggle with cross-file dependency resolution.

## Source Excerpt

"As shown in Figure 4, the dominance of NameError suggests that current LLMs still struggle with cross-file dependency resolution."

## Note

This is an author interpretation of an observed failure pattern rather than a direct causal demonstration.
---

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Table 7
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Removing explicit interface information reduced Lite-set performance: Gemini-3-Pro-Preview fell from 10.0% to 3.3% resolved, and GPT-5.1-Codex fell from 20.0% to 16.7% resolved.

## Source Excerpt

Under "Without Interface" in Table 7, Gemini-3-Pro-Preview shows "3.3 (-6.7)" resolved and GPT-5.1-Codex shows "16.7 (-3.3)"; the comparison baseline in the same table implies 10.0% and 20.0% resolved with interface visible.

## Note

The evidence supports the paper's claim that explicit interfaces materially affect performance.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-10-009
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: metric
citation: Table 7
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.10975
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Providing visible unit tests sharply increased Lite-set performance: GPT-5.1-Codex reached 63.3% resolved and 80.9% passed, while Gemini-3-Pro-Preview reached 60.0% resolved and 80.6% passed.

## Source Excerpt

In Table 7 under "Visible Unit Tests," GPT-5.1-Codex reports "63.3 (+43.3)" resolved and "80.9 (+20.7)" passed, while Gemini-3-Pro-Preview reports "60.0 (+50.0)" resolved and "80.6 (+37.2)" passed.

## Note

This evidence shows the measured effect of giving agents ground-truth unit tests.
```
